title: Casual masculine "let's…"
explanation: The volitional form ending in 〜よう／おう expresses a suggestion or intention ("let's…" / "I'll…"). Adding the sentence-final particle ぜ makes the suggestion confident and assertive, with a distinctly masculine, casual register used among male friends. なろうぜ literally combines なる ("to become") with this pattern, giving a punchy "let's go become…" feel rather than a neutral suggestion. Compared to ぞ, ぜ is the softer of the two assertive masculine particles: ぞ is blunter and rougher (often self-directed), while ぜ is more friendly, used among male friends as a kind of camaraderie marker. Both are far more casual than the polite ましょう.

title: Thought I (knew/was/had)… but
formation: [verb plain form / 〜ている form] + つもりだった
explanation: つもり on its own marks an intention or a belief about oneself. Non-past つもりだ means "I (currently) think/intend X." Past つもりだった shifts the meaning: "I had thought / believed X — but it turned out I was wrong." It almost always sets up a reversal: the speaker reveals their belief was an illusion. 知っていたつもりだった = "I thought I knew" — implying the speaker did not, in fact, know. Native writers reach for this when admitting that book-knowledge or imagined preparation didn't survive contact with reality.

title: Almost did / nearly happened
formation: [verb stem (連用形)] + そうになる
explanation: 〜そうになる describes something that almost happened but didn't — the "near miss" form. It attaches to a verb stem (吐く → 吐きそうになる, "almost threw up"; 泣く → 泣きそうになる, "almost cried"). Distinguish from 〜そうだ ("looks like (it will)"), which is appearance-based and conjectural: 〜そうになる is specifically about an action coming close to occurring without actually doing so. Often combined with 何度〜かわからない ("countless times") or past-tense narration to describe scary moments survived.

title: If only… (counterfactual regret)
explanation: 〜ば...のに combines a hypothetical conditional (〜ば, "if X were the case") with sentence-final のに ("…but it isn't / but it wasn't") to express counterfactual regret: "if only X had been true, then Y wouldn't have happened — but X wasn't, so Y did." The のに can trail off as ……, leaving the regret unfinished. Common in apologies and self-recrimination: 強ければ…なかったのに literally "if (we) had been stronger, (we) wouldn't have made (you worry) — but (we weren't, so we did)." Distinguish from concessive のに ("even though"): the conditional 〜ば in front, plus the past tense of the second clause, signals counterfactual rather than concessive.

title: Even while / despite (concessive)
formation: [verb stem / i-adj / na-adj / noun] + ながらも
explanation: 〜ながら on its own marks two simultaneous actions ("while doing X, doing Y"). Adding the contrastive particle も turns it into a concessive: "even while X, still Y" — where Y is contrary to what X would lead you to expect. 抱えながらも...強い = "even while saddled with [me], they're still strong." Distinguish from plain ながら: ながらも always implies contrast and sounds more written/literary than 〜ても. The two states genuinely co-exist (the subject is doing both X and Y) rather than X being a mere supposition.

title: Even while / despite (literary concessive)
explanation: 〜つつ on its own marks two simultaneous actions, with a written/literary register (interchangeable with ながら in form, but more bookish in feel). Adding the contrastive particle も produces a concessive: "even while X, still Y," where Y goes against what X would lead you to expect. 敵対されつつも...排除し = "even while being opposed (by the Empire), still cleared every obstacle." Distinguish from 〜ながらも: つつも belongs in narration, written speeches, and formal prose, while ながらも is closer to neutral. Both are concessive — neither is a mere simultaneity marker.

title: Rather than… (preference comparison)
formation: [verb plain non-past] + くらいなら + [alternative] + 方がいい
explanation: 〜くらいなら〜方がいい sets up a preference between two undesirable-or-better options: "rather than (going to the lengths of doing X), Y is preferable." The first clause names something the speaker considers bad enough to reject, even if it's not extreme on its own; the くらい ("to the extent of") signals that even at that mild level, X is worse than Y. 合わないハンターと組むくらいなら = "if it's going to be teaming up with a Hunter who doesn't fit (even just that)…" — the ground-floor of unpleasantness. The pattern often appears with a sigh of resignation rather than a strong wish.

title: Far from… / let alone
formation: [noun / verb plain / i-adj / na-adj+な] + どころか
explanation: 〜どころか flips an expected milder claim for a stronger, often opposite one: "far from X — actually Y." The clause before どころか names what someone might assume or what would be the more generous reading; the clause after corrects them with a harsher reality. 仲がいいどころか顔見知りですらない = "far from being on good terms — they aren't even acquaintances." The construction works in both directions (X is bad → Y is worse; X is bad → Y is actually good) but always implies a sharp contrast and surprise. Often pairs with すら / さえ in the second clause for additional emphasis.

title: Can't help but / can't keep from
formation: [verb negative stem (未然形)] + ずにはいられない
explanation: 〜ずにはいられない expresses an irresistible compulsion: "cannot help (doing X) / cannot keep oneself from (doing X)." It builds on the literary negation 〜ず (= 〜ない) with にはいられない ("cannot remain (in such a state)"). 見惚れずにはいられない = "cannot help being transfixed." For する-verbs the form is せずにはいられない (note せず, not しず). The construction is bookish and weightier than the more colloquial 〜ないではいられない, and it's especially common in third-person narration when the writer wants to convey involuntary reaction with a literary flourish.

title: That said / be that as it may
explanation: 〜とはいえ marks a concessive: "although that's true / be that as it may, ~". The clause before sets up something the speaker grants; the clause after pushes back on it. Compared to ても (everyday concessive) and ながらも (literary, contrastive), とはいえ has a dry, written register and is especially common in narration where the writer is acknowledging a point only to overturn it. 如何に〜とはいえ ("however much (it may be) ~") is an extremely common collocation that intensifies the concession.

title: Without (doing) ~
explanation: 〜ことなく is the literary alternative to 〜ないで ("without (doing X)"). The clause it modifies describes an action that happens without the bracketed verb ever occurring. 視線を外すことなく = "without taking (one's) eyes off (it)." Compared to 〜ずに (also "without ~"), ことなく feels more written/literary and is favoured in narration; it cannot be used at the very end of a sentence as the main predicate. The pattern is especially common before continuative verbs (〜し, 〜て) where it modifies a wider clause.

title: Surely not / cannot
formation: [verb dictionary form (godan)] / [verb stem (ichidan)] + まい  ※ する → するまい / すまい / しまい; くる → くるまい / こまい
explanation: 〜まい is a literary negative-volitional / negative-conjecture marker, expressing either "I will not (do X)" (volitional) or "surely (X) will not happen / cannot be the case" (conjecture). The conjecture sense is the more common in modern writing and is roughly equivalent to 〜ないだろう, but with a markedly more literary, often emphatic feel. アークにはそんなことできまい = "Arc surely cannot do that." Often paired with conditional clauses or rhetorical contrasts to lend a final-pronouncement weight, especially in narration and reflective monologue.

title: With (the use of) ~ / if one employs
explanation: 〜をもってすれば is a literary, formal conditional: "if one were to employ ~ / with the use of ~." It builds on をもって ("by means of / with") and the conditional すれば ("if one does"), and is used to assert that some resource — a person's ability, a tool, an authority — is sufficient to handle the matter at hand. ロドルフの怪力と宝具をもってすれば = "with Rodolphe's strength and artefact (deployed), …" The construction belongs almost entirely to written/formal Japanese — official prose, narration, written speeches — and feels stilted in everyday conversation, where 〜があれば or 〜なら would do.

title: Cannot (do X) — moral / circumstantial impossibility
formation: [verb plain non-past] + わけにはいかない
explanation: 〜わけにはいかない expresses an inability to do (X) that comes not from physical impossibility but from circumstance — social, moral, or relational. The speaker could in principle do X, but, given the situation, must not. 師匠の顔に泥を塗るわけにはいかない = "there's no way (I) can shame my master." Distinguish from できない (general inability) and 〜てはいけない (rule-based prohibition): わけにはいかない frames the impossibility as a duty the speaker owes to circumstance. The negative form 〜ないわけにはいかない ("can't not do (X) / have to do (X)") inverts the pattern.

title: About to / starting to (but not yet)
formation: [verb stem (連用形)] + かける / かけた
explanation: 〜かける attached to a verb stem describes an action that has begun or is about to begin but is not completed. 立ち上がりかけた = "started to stand up (but didn't finish)." 言いかける = "started to say (something) / was about to say." Compare 〜始める ("begin to ~," with the action clearly in progress); 〜かける specifically leaves the action arrested, often interrupted by something else. Often paired with another clause that does the interrupting (掴み引き止める, "grabs and stops"). Don't confuse with the unrelated 〜にかける ("with respect to ~ / to bet on ~").

title: On the occasion of / when undertaking
formation: [verb dictionary form / noun] + にあたり (formal) / にあたって (slightly less formal)
explanation: 〜にあたり / 〜にあたって marks a deliberate threshold moment when one undertakes (X) — "on (the occasion of) ~ / when setting out to ~." The pattern attaches to a verb dictionary form or noun and frames the following clause as the action taken at that moment. ハンターを目指すにあたり = "in setting out to aim at being Hunters." Compared to 〜時 (the everyday "when"), にあたり is reserved for weighty, formal, or threshold occasions — entrance ceremonies, project kick-offs, deliberate undertakings — and feels stilted when applied to mundane events. The 〜にあたり form is more written/stiff than 〜にあたって.

title: In order to / so as to (literary)
formation: [verb dictionary form] + べく  ※ する → すべく (preferred) / するべく (accepted)
explanation: 〜べく is a literary purposive: "in order to ~ / so as to ~." The clause it attaches to states a goal; the main clause states the action taken in pursuit of that goal. ハンターとなるべく切磋琢磨した = "trained themselves rigorously in order to become Hunters." Distinguish from 〜ために (everyday "in order to," usable in any register): べく belongs to written, formal, or solemn prose — narration, speeches, written declarations — and feels old-fashioned in casual speech. Note also べくして ("as expected to ~") and べからず (archaic prohibition), which share the same べき root.
